# Etiology and Triggers

The etiology of hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis in adults encompasses a broad and heterogeneous spectrum of triggers, ranging from infections and malignancies to autoimmune disorders and iatrogenic exposures. Identifying the underlying trigger is of paramount importance, as it directly informs treatment strategy and prognosis. Unlike familial HLH, in which defined genetic defects in cytotoxic granule pathways constitute the primary pathogenic mechanism, adult-onset HLH is overwhelmingly acquired, arising when a precipitating insult overwhelms immune regulatory capacity and initiates the characteristic cycle of uncontrolled macrophage and T-cell activation [@Rose2019Recommendations]. Contemporary classification systems recognize four major etiological categories: infection-associated, malignancy-associated, autoimmune-associated (macrophage activation syndrome), and iatrogenic HLH. In clinical practice, these categories may overlap, as patients with underlying malignancy or autoimmune disease may develop HLH in the setting of superimposed infection or immunosuppressive therapy.

## Infection-Associated HLH

Infection is the most frequently identified trigger of acquired HLH in adults, and viral infections predominate among infectious etiologies worldwide. Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) is the single most common viral trigger globally, with a particularly high prevalence in East Asian cohorts, where EBV-driven HLH accounts for a substantial proportion of adult cases [@Rose2019Recommendations; @Mehta2020COVID]. EBV-associated HLH carries a notably poor prognosis, in part because the virus directly infects T cells and NK cells, amplifying the very immune effectors responsible for cytokine storm pathophysiology. Other herpesviruses implicated in HLH include cytomegalovirus (CMV), herpes simplex virus types 1 and 2 (HSV-1/2), human herpesvirus 6 (HHV-6), and human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8, also known as Kaposi sarcoma-associated herpesvirus), the latter being particularly relevant in immunocompromised hosts [@Rose2019Recommendations].

Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) represents both a direct trigger and a predisposing condition for HLH, as HIV-associated immunosuppression increases susceptibility to opportunistic infections that may independently provoke HLH. The emergence of SARS-CoV-2 during the COVID-19 pandemic introduced a novel viral trigger, with multiple case series and cohort studies documenting HLH and HLH-like hyperinflammatory syndromes in severely affected patients [@Mehta2020COVID; @Merad2020Pathological; @Moore2020Cytokine]. The hyperinflammatory state observed in severe COVID-19 shares key features with HLH, including hyperferritinemia, elevated interleukin-6, and macrophage-driven tissue injury, though its complete overlap with classical HLH criteria remains debated [@Webb2020Clinical; @McGonagle2020The; @Ragab2020The]. Other viral triggers of clinical significance include influenza, parvovirus B19, dengue, and adenovirus, each of which has been documented in case reports and small series [@Soy2020Cytokine].

Bacterial infections constitute an important and often underrecognized category of HLH triggers. Mycobacterium tuberculosis is a leading bacterial cause, particularly in endemic regions and immunocompromised populations. Other bacterial pathogens reported to trigger HLH include Salmonella species, Staphylococcus aureus, and Streptococcus pneumoniae, as well as Brucella species in regions where brucellosis is endemic. Tick-borne organisms merit particular attention: Ehrlichia, Rickettsia, and Anaplasma species have all been associated with HLH, and clinicians in endemic areas should maintain a high index of suspicion during tick season [@Rose2019Recommendations].

Fungal infections represent an additional trigger category with disproportionate representation among immunocompromised patients. Histoplasma capsulatum is the most commonly reported fungal trigger, followed by Candida species, Cryptococcus neoformans, and Aspergillus species. Parasitic infections, though less frequent in high-income settings, are important triggers worldwide: Plasmodium species (particularly P. falciparum), Toxoplasma gondii, Babesia species, and Leishmania species (visceral leishmaniasis) have all been documented as HLH triggers, with Leishmania-associated HLH carrying a particularly distinctive clinical presentation that may mimic lymphoma [@Rose2019Recommendations; @Shakoory2016Interleukin].

## Malignancy-Associated HLH

Malignancy is the second most common trigger of HLH in adults and carries the highest mortality among etiological categories. Hematological malignancies dominate, with T-cell and NK-cell lymphomas representing the most frequently implicated neoplasms. The strong association between T/NK-cell lymphomas and HLH reflects the direct involvement of the malignant lymphocyte population in the dysregulated immune activation that drives the syndrome. Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL), Hodgkin lymphoma, and various leukemias (including acute lymphoblastic and acute myeloid leukemia) are also recognized triggers, though at lower frequency than T/NK-cell lineage malignancies [@Rose2019Recommendations; @Emile2016Revised].

Distinguishing malignancy-associated HLH from infection-associated HLH is clinically critical, as treatment of the underlying malignancy is essential for disease control. The sIL-2R to ferritin ratio has emerged as a valuable discriminatory biomarker: a ratio greater than 8.6 is suggestive of lymphoma-associated HLH, compared with a mean ratio of approximately 0.7 in non-malignancy-associated cases. This reflects the disproportionate T-cell activation and sIL-2R production characteristic of lymphoma-driven disease relative to the macrophage-predominant ferritin elevation seen in infection-triggered HLH [@Rose2019Recommendations]. Multicentric Castleman disease, a lymphoproliferative disorder driven by interleukin-6 overproduction, represents a clinically important overlap entity that can trigger or mimic HLH, and should be considered in the differential diagnosis of patients presenting with concurrent lymphadenopathy, hypercytokinemia, and HLH features [@Tanaka2016Immunotherapeutic].

## Macrophage Activation Syndrome

Macrophage activation syndrome (MAS) represents HLH occurring in the context of autoimmune and autoinflammatory disease. Although MAS is most extensively characterized in systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis (sJIA), it is increasingly recognized as a complication of multiple adult rheumatic conditions. Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is the most commonly reported adult autoimmune trigger, followed by adult-onset Still disease (AOSD), rheumatoid arthritis, systemic vasculitis, and inflammatory bowel disease [@Crayne2019The; @Fanouriakis2021Update]. National database analyses have demonstrated that approximately 6.2% of hospitalizations for AOSD are complicated by MAS, with dramatic implications for outcomes: in-hospital mortality rises from 1.5% in AOSD without MAS to 9% when MAS develops [@Crayne2019The; @Shakoory2016Interleukin]. The pathogenesis of MAS in autoimmune disease involves amplification of the pre-existing inflammatory state, with inflammasome activation and interleukin-18 overproduction playing a particularly prominent role in AOSD- and sJIA-associated MAS [@Kaplanski2018Interleukin; @Crayne2019The]. Diagnostic criteria for MAS in sJIA, as defined by the 2016 EULAR/ACR/PRINTO classification, employ lower laboratory thresholds than standard HLH-2004 criteria, reflecting the elevated inflammatory baseline against which MAS develops in patients with active autoimmune disease [@Ravelli2016].

## Iatrogenic HLH

Iatrogenic HLH has emerged as an increasingly important clinical entity with the expansion of immunotherapeutic approaches in oncology. Three principal categories of iatrogenic triggers are recognized: chimeric antigen receptor T-cell (CAR-T) therapy, immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs), and conventional pharmacological agents.

CAR-T cell therapy can precipitate HLH through mechanisms that are pathophysiologically related to, yet clinically distinct from, cytokine release syndrome (CRS). It is essential to distinguish CRS from immune effector cell-associated hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis-like syndrome (IEC-HS), a recently defined entity characterized by delayed onset following CAR-T infusion, often recurring or recrudescing after initial CRS has resolved [@Neelapu2018Chimeric; @Brudno2019Recent]. IEC-HS typically presents with persistent or recrudescent fevers, progressive cytopenias, markedly elevated ferritin, hepatic dysfunction, and coagulopathy occurring days to weeks after CRS onset, distinguishing it temporally and clinically from the acute inflammatory surge of CRS itself. This complication has been reported across multiple approved CAR-T products, including axicabtagene ciloleucel, lisocabtagene maraleucel, brexucabtagene autoleucel, and ciltacabtagene autoleucel [@Locke2019Longterm; @Berdeja2021Ciltacabtagene; @Nastoupil2020Standardofcare; @Cordas2024A]. Recognition of IEC-HS as a distinct entity from CRS is critical, as its management may require HLH-directed therapy including corticosteroids, ruxolitinib, or etoposide rather than tocilizumab alone [@Kotch2019Tocilizumab; @YakoubAgha2020Management].

Immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy with agents such as pembrolizumab, nivolumab, and the combination of nivolumab plus ipilimumab has been associated with HLH as a rare but potentially fatal immune-related adverse event [@RamosCasals2020Immunerelated; @Motzer2019Nivolumab]. The median time to onset of ICI-associated HLH is approximately 102 days from treatment initiation, and reported mortality rates range from 13.7% to 15.3%, underscoring the severity of this complication [@RamosCasals2020Immunerelated]. Combination checkpoint blockade with anti-CTLA-4 and anti-PD-1 agents carries a higher risk than monotherapy, consistent with the greater degree of immune disinhibition achieved by dual checkpoint blockade [@Daver2019Efficacy].

Conventional pharmacological agents constitute a third category of iatrogenic triggers. Antiepileptic drugs, including lamotrigine, carbamazepine, and phenytoin, are among the most commonly implicated medications, likely through drug hypersensitivity mechanisms that overlap with drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS). Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) and sulfasalazine have also been reported as triggers, highlighting the role of sulfonamide hypersensitivity in HLH pathogenesis [@Jamilloux2020Should; @Jamilloux2019JAK]. Recognition of drug-triggered HLH is essential, as withdrawal of the offending agent is a necessary first step in management and may be sufficient in mild cases.
